course_title: Human Development and Relationships Across the Life Span
course_description:
This course is designed to prepare you for clinical practice, and to develop therapeutic skills in your field of study. The course explores mental health treatment in the context of human development, relationships and culture. You will critically analyse, evaluate and develop your knowledge and skills in these areas. You will also be required to develop a critique of your own development, and how this informs your sensitivity in working with clients at different stages of development. The focus is on the integration of theory and practice and the application of generalist and specialist skills within a human developmental, relational and cultural context.

This foundational course lays a strong groundwork for further study and professional practice in mental health, psychotherapy, and art therapy. Central to any mental health encounter is understanding a person’s development, relationships, cultural experience, and identity. Equally important is being attuned to our own sense of self to provide ethical, client-focused care.

Throughout this course, we will explore human life across all developmental stages, examining how culture, identity, and relationships influence who we are, how we view the world, and how we experience mental health concerns. The course emphasises a multicultural, trauma-informed, and social model approach to care, affirming consumer perspectives within the mental health system.

Students will actively engage with these areas through online activities, group sessions, weekly readings, and video content. These resources will facilitate a critical examination of various theories and concepts related to human experience and mental health. Weekly group tutorials will encourage debate and reflection, incorporating our own human experiences into all learning activities.

We look forward to working with you and supporting your journey in this vital field.

UQ Public Timetable

Additional timetable information
Workshops:
In week 3 of Semester you will attend several on campus group workshops, including:
Cultural Humility in Practice - Tuesday 12th August 8:30-12pm
Identity and Inclusive Mental Health Practice - Tuesday 12th August 1-4:30pm
Child and Adolescent Therapy - Thursday 14th August 8:30-12pm
These in person workshops are part of a week long residential suite of workshops, and are compulsory for all students in PXMH7104. Pre-reading and viewing of the Cultural Humility workshop materials is required prior to the workshop - to support discussion and experiential activities.ﾠ
If you have any concerns please contact Course Coordinator Dani Walsh and your relevant Stream Coordinator (Psychotherapy: Jaya Valvoi; Art Therapy: Louise Leotta) to discuss further. Please note attendance hours in this course are a requirement for future registration and accreditation with peak bodies.ﾠ
Tutorials:
Tutorials are weekly (except for week 3), and begin in week 1. Set readings, videos and multimedia content accompany each tutorial topic to broaden and deepen reflective thinking into areas of human development and identity. This content are required to have been read/viewed prior to the group tutorial, to inspire rich discussion of the weeks focus topic. You are encouraged to discuss real life applications of learning and where possible offer experiential examples in class to fellow students.
Times/Days:
Psychotherapy tutorial
: Wednesdays 6:45-8pm with Andrew Munnings via Zoom.
Art Therapy tutorial (local group)
: Mondays 1-2:15pm with Tanya Burkhardt via Zoom
Art Therapy tutorial (remote group)
: Mondays 2:30-3:45pm with Tanya Burkhardt via Zoom
Zoom links will be shared via Blackboard announcement at the start of the semester
AEST (Brisbane) - daylight savings commences in some Australian States and Territories on October 5th 2025.
Please email the Course Coordinator (Dani Walsh - danielle.walsh@uq.edu.au) if you are uncertain which tutorial you should attend once class times have been confirmed via Learn UQ.
course_aims: This course aims to provide students with knowledge and experience that will encourage person focused, culturally sensitive and effective mental health practice and enable students to deliver quality mental health care with people across the lifespan.

learning_outcomes.description: Identify, understand and apply critical analysis to selected key theories of human development as they apply to the field of study
Demonstrate specialist skills and knowledge required to work with clients mental health challenges throughout the lifespan.
Understand the importance of human relationships and the broader socio-cultural environment, and how they can either enhance or impact negatively on mental health.
Understand the importance of developing cultural perspectives and learning to apply cultural sensitivity and humility when working with clients.

assessment_summary.due_date: 18/08/2025 - 30/10/2025; Dates will be confirmed by your Lecturer in Weeks 1-2 of Semester.

assessment_summary.title: Mental Health Challenges Across the Lifespan

assessment_details.title: Leading Tutorial Activity
assessment_details.due_date: 18/08/2025 - 30/10/2025 Dates will be confirmed by your Lecturer in Weeks 1-2 of Semester.
assessment_details.task_description: Each student will have the opportunity to lead the class group in a 10–15-minute activity or therapeutic directive that is relevant to the weekly topic. This activity should be designed to inspire and prompt discussion relating to the week’s topic. This will be followed by a 5-minute Q&A relating to your activity. Examples could include; Directing an activity you might use in practice with a client or consumer from a particular demographic Leading a reflective exercise based on one of the weeks learning resources Facilitating a therapeutic directive which applies learning from the readings in practice, etc. This should not be a summary of the topic/weeks learning resources, but is an opportunity to demonstrate and share therapeutic skills with your peers, and initiate the weeks discussion on the topic and/or learning resources. This assessment leads well into your third assessment ( Mental Health Challenges Across the Lifespan ), where you engage in a similar process in greater depth. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Criteria and Marking You are able to choose, design or adapt an activity or therapeutic directive which is relevant to the weekly topic. 10% You are able to facilitate this activity or therapeutic directive, including integrating the intended learning as it relates to the weekly topic. 10% You are able to respond to questions relating to the activity you have chosen, including justifying your clinical reasoning for this, and your experience facilitating this activity. 10% Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Family Experiences and Therapeutic Practice
assessment_details.task_description: Part One: Family Genogram/Community Map (5%) You are asked to create a family genogram and/or a map of your family/community to reflect on key relationships and family experiences. This visual representation should include relevant key relationships and, where possible, encompass two to three generations of family members. Use standard symbols and elements of a genogram or an alternative mapping method to illustrate the structure and connections within your family/community. Part Two: Reflective Essay (15%) In a 1400-word essay , reflect on how your family of origin, family experiences, and key relationships have shaped your identity and worldview. Articulate key themes, challenges, and learnings that have influenced your life and professional practice in mental health. Address the following points in your reflection: Influence on Therapeutic Practice: Discuss how your family, cultural, and community experiences and relationships have shaped your childhood, development, and worldview. Explain how these experiences influence your therapeutic practice with individuals and families. Connection to Self: Articulate the connection between your experiences and your current sense of self and way of being. State the boundaries of confidentiality and safety you used when deciding what information to include in your essay. Impact on Professional Values: Reflect on how your family, culture, community, and relationships have impacted your professional values and practice in mental health. Present both the strengths and challenges that your experiences bring to your therapeutic practice and alliance. Your essay should be self-reflective and well-written, adhering to the word limit. You may include reflective art pieces or photography related to the topic to guide your written reflections. No references are required. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

Assessments submitted after the due date without an approved extension will receive a late penalty .

assessment_details.title: Mental Health Challenges Across the Lifespan
assessment_details.task_description: Part One: Therapeutic Resource Select a developmental period and target population discussed in the course, and create a therapeutic resource addressing a mental health challenge experienced by this population. Possible formats include a pamphlet, fact sheet, video, animation, podcast, poster, or worksheets. The resource should provide both psychoeducation and therapeutic strategies suitable for consumers or carers during mental health support. Criteria: 1. Developmental Stage and Context: Define the chosen developmental stage and provide a contemporary perspective on its relevance to mental health and well-being. 2. Target Population and Objectives: Specify the target population within the developmental stage and state the intended audience and goals/objectives of the resource. 3. Mental Health Challenge: Focus on one mental health challenge relevant to the target population. Explain its key components and implications on mental health and well-being, considering bio-psychosocial, cultural, relational, and developmental factors. Reference contemporary understandings of this issue in Australian society. 4. Learning and Strategies: Provide learning materials and strategies that support and improve the well-being of the target population in the context of the discussed mental health challenge. Include at least two relevant activities or therapeutic processes, detailing the practical steps and any limitations or caveats. 5. Format and Presentation: Ensure the content, whether read, spoken, or watched, is a minimum of 4 minutes but no longer than 5 minutes. The resource should be in a consumer-friendly format suitable for the intended audience. Use appropriate in-text/spoken referencing of literature/theories where relevant. Part Two: Written Guide Accompany your therapeutic resource with a written guide (maximum 1000 words) intended for other therapists/health professionals. This guide should assist them in using the resource with their consumers/carers. Criteria : 1. Context and Purpose: Detail the context and purpose of the resource, including the key concepts and theories of development utilised. 2. Evidence and Application: Provide evidence for the activities/therapeutic processes shared in the resource and explain their potential usage in practice. 3. Appropriate Writing: Ensure the guide is written appropriately for other therapists and health professionals. Include a bibliography/reference list of literature and other sources used. Submission Instructions Compile both your resource and written guide into one file for submission via Turnitin. For audio/video files, save the file in your University One Drive folder or a similar electronic drive and include a link in your submitted PDF/Word document. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Culture Humility and Self in the Therapeutic Alliance Introduction to PXMH7104, Culture Humility and Self in the Therapeutic Alliance. Preparation for Presentation in Week 3. Why is understanding human development and relationships important for therapists and mental health practitioners? In what ways is it important that we understand our own experience and culture as we work in therapy and mental health? How do we negotiate the connection and alliance between the person and our own human self? How does culture and our upbringing influence our work with others? Learning outcomes: L03, L04

Identity, Gender, Sexuality and Belonging How does my assigned gender and experienced gender differ or match? How do norms or perceptions impact my mental health and sense of self? How does my personal identity interact or fit with those of my family, community and society at large? What does belonging mean and what happens when I don’t belong? How does my identity (or discrimination for my identity) impact my mental health, and in what contexts and why? How much do I know about the needs of LGBTIQA+ people and who I can support them as a therapist? Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04

Workshops/Discussion Topics - Residential Week Active learning about key themes in the course, Cultural Humility, Identity, Inclusive Practice and Child Art/Therapy for all students. Resources and pre-workshop videos listed on Learn.UQ /Learning Resources. Full day Group workshops on Tuesday 12th August and Half Day (AM) workshop Thursday 14th August during residential week (Herston Campus). Please watch the Cultural Humility video (approx. 1 hr) and Child Art Therapy presentation delivered by the UQ team. Please also prepare for participation in the morning workshop - details will be provided to you via email and in your tutorials. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Family and Relationship Structures How much do I know and understand about my own family and its impact on me and my work? How are other families organised? How does this impact on their strengths and vulnerabilities? What about being taken away from your family? Working with the complexity of family and family/community structures in mental health – what are key skills and perspectives? Learning outcomes: L01, L03

Infancy, Attachment, Parenting How does our experience of early relationships with our caregivers influence our development, our sense of self, our world view, our interpersonal skills and sensitivities? How does attachment and early relationships impact on children and impact on humans in later life? How do we view Development within the infant, and even pre -birth, and the link with relationships. How can we understand this time of life e.g. Attachment, Parenting Style, Caregiver mental health? Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04

Early Childhood How do I focus on learning, being my own person but needing closeness? What are A.C.E.’s and how do they manifest in childhood and throughout life? How do developmental issues start to arise and be seen by those around me? What if I experience a trauma or significant change, how does this effect my development? How do we support young children in this period? What mental health strengths and needs or concerns arise at this stage? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Middle Childhood The time where we are going to school, making friends, learning and exploring the world, learning about who we are and what we like, taking on new responsibilities. What does wellness and mental health look like? What challenges might arise? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Adolescence Who am I, where do I fit in relationship to others within family, culture and rest of world? Where is my life going? What am I passionate about? How does society and our parents see us? So much is changing, excitement and stress all at once. What is the key to wellness and positive mental health? How do we manage the risks of mental health, self harm and suicidality that arise? How do we support young people, families and the wider community? How do we engage a recovery model when an adolescent in is tension between being cared for and being independent? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Early Adulthood to Adulthood Assessment 3 Check in Transitioning to the adult world and adult responsibilities. What am I going to do with my life and who am I going to do it with? Shifts in family and friend relationships, entering the workforce and a career. Intimate relationships, parenting and lots of decisions. How does our society see this period of life, what is important, what are the key stressors? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Middle to Mature Adulthood Changes and transitions of relationships, looking forward and back. Have I done what I want/need to do at home and in the world? Family, career, changes in the world emerge and how do I see these, manage these? What does mental health look like as I go through different parts of adulthood? How do my strengths, needs, hopes and goals sustain or change or evolve? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Old Age How do I engage with what’s done and what’s to come? Reflections of my life and on future death. Retirement, connections, transitions in lifestyle. What challenges occur at this age, what benefits of being older and having lived and experienced a lifetime of relationships and situations? What does wellness look like? What mental health concerns become relevant? How does my identity, culture influence my perspective? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

End of Life End of life can occur when you are in old age or much earlier due to illness or other concerns. How does being near the end of our life impact our sense of self, the world and our mental health and wellness. How can therapists support individuals, families and communities in situations of palliative care, life limited conditions, enduring suicidality, grief and loss. What does life and death mean in my culture? How does my family engage in or understand end of life and beyond? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Emotional and Social Development How do I prefer to “be” in society, in my community, how do I prefer to interact with others? What challenges do I face in social settings and why? Am I an introvert, extrovert, a social butterfly, a quiet achiever, an observer but what do these terms mean anyway? How do I perceive care, love, support from others? How does this impact my coping, my perception of mental health, my expression of resiliency, my sense of self? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
